name: France
summary: A top-3 European AI hub (Mistral AI, Meta FAIR Paris, Station F, Datadog) with dual citizenship retained both ways, a full Campus France/embassy pipeline in Dhaka, universal PUMA healthcare, a strong passport and Europe's largest Muslim community with abundant mosques and halal — but tempered for a visibly Muslim couple by France's laïcité regime (headscarf bans in schools/sport, the 2023 abaya ban, 700+ mosque closures), a powerful Rassemblement National (largest single party) amid a hung-parliament instability, a 58% marginal tax wedge, GPI-2025 unease (74th), and a B2-French citizenship bar raised in 2026. Derived overall sits just below the 60% inclusion line.

links.title: France-Visas — official visa portal (Bangladesh)
Campus France — official (study in France)

job-market.summary: Paris is a top-tier European AI hub (Mistral AI, Meta FAIR, Hugging Face roots, Station F, Datadog) within a large multi-city tech market, though French-HQ pay is moderate and French often helps.

pros.text: Paris is one of Europe's leading AI hubs — Mistral AI, Meta FAIR Paris, Station F, Datadog (~800 Paris engineers) — with deep demand in Python/MLOps/LLM.
Large multi-city market (Paris, Lyon, Toulouse, Grenoble); the Passeport Talent route favours qualified tech/AI hires, and US-HQ offices pay €120k–180k senior.

cons.text: French-HQ salaries are moderate (Paris SWE ~€52k, senior ~€75k–95k) versus US-HQ outliers.
French is commonly expected outside big-tech/startup roles, narrowing the market for a new arrival.

links.title: Jobicy — AI Engineer salary, France (2026)
Euro Top Tech — Software Engineer salary France (2026)

visa-access.summary: A clear strength versus other EU options: France runs a full Campus France pipeline plus an embassy and VFS in Dhaka, so Bangladeshi students apply and give biometrics in-country, with modest funds requirements.

pros.text: France has a full embassy, an established Campus France office and a VFS centre in Dhaka — Bangladeshi students apply and give biometrics in-country (no travel to New Delhi), with processing ~30–60 days.
Funds proof is modest (~€615/month) and French is not required for the visa for English-taught programmes.

cons.text: The Campus France 'Études en France' process adds a mandatory academic interview plus embassy and visa-section interviews.
The broader French migration climate is tightening, raising discretion.

links.title: France-Visas — Bangladesh (official)
Campus France — Études en France procedure (official)

citizenship.summary: Dual citizenship is retained both ways and the passport is elite (Henley 4th), with a 5-year path — but from 2026 naturalisation requires B2 French and a civics exam, and processing runs 18–24 months.

pros.text: France recognises dual nationality with no renunciation, and France is on Bangladesh's DNC list, so a naturalising Bangladeshi keeps BD nationality.
Naturalisation is possible after 5 years' habitual residence; the French passport ranks joint 4th on Henley 2026 (~185).

cons.text: From 1 January 2026 candidates must demonstrate B2 French (up from B1) plus pass a new civics/integration exam and show stable French-source income.
Processing typically runs 18–24 months with préfecture discretion.

links.title: Fragomen — France: civics and language tests now required (2026)

post-study-work.summary: Master's graduates get a 12-month APS with full work rights for any employer, converting to a Salarié or Passeport Talent permit — a short but open runway, available to each graduating spouse.

pros.text: A master's from a French institution grants a 12-month APS with full-time work rights for any employer (no separate work permit), and each graduating spouse can hold their own.
The APS converts to a Salarié permit or the favourable Passeport Talent for higher-skilled tech/AI roles.

cons.text: The APS is only 12 months (shorter than Canada/Denmark), and 2026 rules require A2 French to convert and bar using the APS to remote-work for a non-French company.

links.title: IAS — France post-study work APS guide

spouse-family.summary: The Passeport Talent route gives family members work rights and children free schooling, and if both spouses study each holds an independent track.

pros.text: The Passeport Talent 'famille' permit gives the accompanying spouse work rights, and children attend public school free of charge.
If both spouses study, each holds their own permit and APS route.

cons.text: Family reunification for a student principal carries means conditions, and the dependent spouse's work access during the study phase is more limited than on the Talent route.

links.title: France-Visas — official portal

msc-study.summary: Deep English-taught CS/AI options (PSL, Polytechnique, Paris-Saclay, Sorbonne) at historically low public tuition, with 60% of priority grants reserved for AI — though non-EU fees rise from September 2026.

pros.text: 52 English-taught AI master's nationwide at top institutions (PSL, Polytechnique, Paris-Saclay, Sorbonne); public-university tuition has been low and 60% of priority grants are reserved for AI.
A Bangladeshi BSc-CSE is admissible via Campus France; students may work ~964 hrs/yr (~20 hrs/week), and a master qualifies for the APS.

cons.text: France is directing universities to charge higher non-EU tuition from September 2026, and elite grandes écoles are expensive.

links.title: Mastersportal — AI master's in France (2026)
ICEF Monitor — France to raise non-EU tuition from Sept 2026

pr-pathway.summary: Permanent residence (carte de résident) is reachable after 5 years, with the Passeport Talent easing the route, but 2026 added civic/language tests and préfecture friction is real.

pros.text: A carte de résident (10-year) is reachable after 5 years, and the Passeport Talent provides a clearer multi-year track for skilled workers.

cons.text: 2026 introduced civic and language tests for multi-year permits and resident cards, and préfecture processing is slow and discretionary.

links.title: Fragomen — France civics/language tests for residence cards (2026)

income-cost.summary: Decent Paris tech salaries are eaten by a heavy tax/social wedge and Paris housing, leaving a moderate surplus; provincial cities are cheaper.

pros.text: Low public tuition and CAF housing aid help the study phase, and provincial tech cities (Lyon, Toulouse, Grenoble) are markedly cheaper than Paris.

cons.text: A heavy tax/social wedge (58% marginal) and expensive Paris housing compress the dual-earner surplus.

links.title: OECD — Taxing Wages 2025: France country note (PDF)

healthcare.summary: Universal PUMA coverage after a short qualifying period (students enrol in social security), high quality, with strong family benefits — a clear strength.

pros.text: Protection Universelle Maladie (PUMA) gives residents universal coverage after ~3 months; international students enrol in the French social-security health scheme (free).
High-quality care plus strong family benefits (subsidised crèches, allocations familiales).

cons.text: A mutuelle top-up is usual for full reimbursement, and there is a short qualifying period before PUMA applies.

links.title: Campus France — health insurance for students (official)

culture-language.summary: French is essential for daily life and status (B2 for citizenship from 2026), and while big-tech works in English, the laïcité climate makes acceptance of visible Muslim practice difficult.

pros.text: Excellent work-life balance (35-hour week, long holidays) and a rich culture; big-tech/startup workplaces operate in English, and a large South-Asian/Maghrebi diaspora eases landing.

cons.text: French is essential for daily life and status (B2 for citizenship from 2026), with a mandatory integration contract (CIR).
The laïcité framework restricts visible Muslim practice, complicating social acceptance for a hijab-wearing newcomer.

links.title: Campus France — official

safety-law.summary: Mid-tier rule of law (WJP 2025: 22nd), but France ranks low for peace in Western Europe (GPI 2025: 74th) given militarisation, civil unrest and urban crime, with laïcité carve-outs in anti-discrimination.

pros.text: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: 22nd of 143 — solid institutions and civil justice, with an active anti-discrimination ombudsman (Défenseur des droits).

cons.text: Global Peace Index 2025: 74th — the lowest-ranked Western European state (driven by militarisation, external conflicts, civil unrest and urban crime).
Anti-discrimination protection is undercut by laïcité carve-outs targeting religious dress, and banlieue marginalisation persists.

links.title: World Justice Project — France ranks 22nd (2025 profile)
Global Peace Index 2025 (PDF)

politics.summary: A hung-parliament instability with the far-right Rassemblement National as the largest single party, and laïcité weaponised across the mainstream against visible Islam — a hostile climate for a Muslim newcomer.

pros.text: Skilled migration channels (Passeport Talent) remain open and AI is a stated national priority.

cons.text: The Rassemblement National is the largest single party (142 seats, 2024) amid a hung parliament and rapid PM turnover, with immigration law in flux.
Laïcité is weaponised across the political mainstream against visible Islam — school hijab/abaya bans, proposals to ban the hijab for under-15s and in sport, and 700+ mosque closures.

links.title: 2024 French legislative election (overview)
France 24 — politicians accused of weaponising laïcité against Muslims

tax.summary: A very heavy tax/social wedge (58% marginal, 3rd-highest OECD); the impatriate regime is generous but largely unavailable to graduates who became resident while studying, with a France–Bangladesh treaty in the broad French network.

pros.text: The family-quotient (quotient familial) system can favour couples/families, and France's broad treaty network includes a France–Bangladesh double-taxation convention.

cons.text: The marginal tax/social wedge is 58.2% (3rd-highest OECD), and the generous impatriate regime generally requires recruitment from abroad — largely unavailable to a graduate who became resident while studying in France.

links.title: OECD — Taxing Wages 2026: France country note

muslim-diaspora.summary: Europe's largest Muslim community with abundant mosques/halal and a real Bangladeshi quarter in Paris (La Chapelle) — but the worst legal climate in Europe for visible practice (school/sport hijab and abaya bans, mosque closures).

pros.text: France has Europe's largest Muslim population (~6m, ~8–10%) with abundant mosques and halal everywhere, and a real Bangladeshi quarter in Paris (La Chapelle / 'Little Bangladesh') with a rich South-Asian ecosystem.

cons.text: The laïcité regime is the most restrictive in Europe for visible Muslim practice — hijab banned in schools/public sector and sport, the 2023 abaya ban, the niqab/burqa ban, halal-meal and slaughter targeting, and 700+ mosque closures since 2020.

links.title: Bridge Initiative — Timeline of France's targeting of Muslim women's dress
